runs.interpretation: Medium-run elasticity of taxable income for high-income U.S. taxpayers, combining both real behavioral responses and avoidance/shifting responses to a 1% change in the net-of-tax rate.
citations: Feldstein (1995, 1999) - estimated ETIs of 1.0–3.0 for high-income earners using TRA86, widely cited as upper-bound estimates
Gruber & Saez (2002) - influential study finding ETI ~0.4 for broad income, ~0.57 for taxable income, with higher estimates (~0.5–0.7) for high earners
Saez, Slemrod & Giertz (2012, JEL) - survey concluding central ETI estimate around 0.25 overall, but noting top-earner estimates often 0.5–1.0 with wide uncertainty
runs.reasoningSummary: The ETI literature for high-income U.S. taxpayers shows substantial variation. Early Feldstein estimates using TRA86 suggested very high elasticities (1.0+), but later work controlling for mean reversion and income trends revised these downward. Gruber & Saez (2002) found ~0.57 for taxable income overall, with higher values for top earners. The Saez-Slemrod-Giertz survey cautions that many estimates are contaminated by income shifting and non-tax trends. For high earners in the medium run (combining real and avoidance responses), a central estimate around 0.5 seems reasonable, with genuine uncertainty spanning roughly 0.3–0.8 for the interquartile range. The upper tail extends to ~1.4 reflecting studies that find large short-run or avoidance-driven spikes.

Early Feldstein estimates using TRA86 suggested very high elasticities (1.0+), but later work controlling for mean reversion and income trends revised these downward. Gruber & Saez (2002) found ~0.57 for taxable income overall, with higher values for top earners. The Saez-Slemrod-Giertz survey cautions that many estimates are contaminated by income shifting and non-tax trends. For high earners in the medium run (combining real and avoidance responses), a central estimate around 0.5 seems reasonable, with genuine uncertainty spanning roughly 0.3\u20130.8 for the interquartile range. The upper tail extends to ~1.4 reflecting studies that find large short-run or avoidance-driven spikes."}

runs.interpretation: Medium-run elasticity of taxable income for high-income U.S. taxpayers, combining both real behavioral responses and avoidance/sheltering responses to a 1% change in the net-of-tax rate.
citations: Feldstein (1995, 1999) - estimated ETI around 1.0–1.5 for high earners using 1986 Tax Reform Act variation
Saez, Slemrod, and Giertz (2012, JEL) - survey concluding ETI likely between 0.12 and 0.40 for broad population, higher for top earners, cautioning about income-shifting vs. real responses
Chetty (2012) - distinguishing optimization frictions and noting that broad ETI estimates may overstate efficiency costs; top-earner ETI often estimated 0.5–1.0 in older studies
runs.reasoningSummary: The ETI literature is wide-ranging. Early estimates by Feldstein using the 1986 TRA found very high elasticities (1.0–1.5) for top earners, but subsequent work (Saez, Slemrod, Giertz 2012) argued these were inflated by income-shifting and transitory effects. Controlling for mean reversion and base-broadening, medium-run estimates for top earners typically fall in the 0.3–0.8 range when avoidance and real responses are combined. I place my median at 0.5, reflecting the center of the credible range for high-income U.S. taxpayers in a medium-run window. The upper tail extends to ~1.2 to account for studies using older data or specific high-income subgroups where avoidance channels are very elastic.

Controlling for mean reversion and base-broadening, medium-run estimates for top earners typically fall in the 0.3\u20130.8 range when avoidance and real responses are combined. I place my median at 0.5, reflecting the center of the credible range for high-income U.S. taxpayers in a medium-run window. The upper tail extends to ~1.2 to account for studies using older data or specific high-income subgroups where avoidance channels are very elastic."}

runs.interpretation: Medium-run elasticity of taxable income for high-income U.S. taxpayers, combining both real behavioral responses and avoidance/sheltering responses to changes in the net-of-tax rate.
citations: Feldstein (1995, 1999) - estimated ETIs of ~1.0–1.5 for top earners using 1986 TRA variation
Saez, Slemrod, and Giertz (2012, Journal of Economic Literature) - survey concluding central ETI estimates around 0.25 for broad population but higher for top earners, with wide uncertainty
Chetty (2012, AEJ Policy) - argued ETI for real responses much lower (~0.1–0.3) and that avoidance responses dominate among top earners
runs.reasoningSummary: The ETI literature for high-income U.S. taxpayers is dominated by large estimates from early work (Feldstein 1995, 1999) using the 1986 Tax Reform Act, yielding ETIs of 1–2, but these were later critiqued for mean-reversion and income-shifting biases. More careful identification (Saez et al. 2012 survey) suggests the broad-population ETI is around 0.25, but top earners systematically show higher elasticities due to greater tax-planning flexibility and income-form shifting. For the medium-run combining real and avoidance channels, a plausible central estimate for top earners is around 0.5, with substantial uncertainty—some credible estimates fall below 0.3 and others above 1.0 depending on methodology and tax reform studied. I set p50 at 0.5 reflecting the center of the informed post-critique range for this specific subgroup.

More careful identification (Saez et al. 2012 survey) suggests the broad-population ETI is around 0.25, but top earners systematically show higher elasticities due to greater tax-planning flexibility and income-form shifting. For the medium-run combining real and avoidance channels, a plausible central estimate for top earners is around 0.5, with substantial uncertainty\u2014some credible estimates fall below 0.3 and others above 1.0 depending on methodology and tax reform studied. I set p50 at 0.5 reflecting the center of the informed post-critique range for this specific subgroup."}

runs.interpretation: Medium-run elasticity of taxable income for high-income U.S. taxpayers, combining both real behavioral responses and avoidance/sheltering responses to a 1% change in the net-of-tax rate.
citations: Feldstein (1995, 1999) - early high ETI estimates (~1.0–3.0) using ERTA and TRA86 variation, influential but later critiqued for income-shifting
Saez, Slemrod & Giertz (2012, JEL) - survey concluding ETI likely between 0.1–0.5 for broad population, higher for top earners with avoidance included
Chetty (2012) - bounds on structural ETI suggesting avoidance raises observed ETI well above real labor-supply elasticity for top earners
runs.reasoningSummary: The ETI literature for high-income U.S. taxpayers shows wide dispersion depending on methodology and time horizon. Early work (Feldstein) found very high elasticities (1.0+), but subsequent research controlling for mean reversion and income shifting revised these downward. For the medium run combining real and avoidance responses at the top of the income distribution, the Saez-Slemrod-Giertz review and related work suggest a central estimate around 0.4–0.6, with substantial uncertainty on both sides. High-end estimates (0.8–1.3) are plausible if avoidance channels are broad; lower estimates (0.1–0.3) apply if only real responses or stricter identification is used. I place the median at 0.5, reflecting that top earners have more avoidance options than the general population, pushing their ETI above the ~0.2–0.3 often cited for the overall population.

runs.interpretation: Medium-run elasticity of taxable income for high-income U.S. taxpayers, combining both real behavioral responses and avoidance/sheltering responses to a 1% change in the net-of-tax rate.
citations: Feldstein (1995, 1999) - estimated ETI around 1.0–1.5 for high-income taxpayers using 1986 Tax Reform Act variation
Gruber & Saez (2002) - estimated ETI of ~0.57 for broad income, higher (~0.57–0.67) for top earners, widely cited benchmark

runs.reasoningSummary: The ETI literature shows substantial uncertainty, especially for high-income taxpayers where avoidance margins are larger. Early Feldstein estimates were very high (1.0–1.5), but later work with better controls (Gruber & Saez 2002; Saez et al. 2012) suggested lower values around 0.4–0.7 for top earners in the medium run. The combined real-plus-avoidance ETI for high-income U.S. taxpayers is generally estimated in the 0.3–0.8 range in the modern literature, with meaningful uncertainty on both sides. I center at 0.5 as a reasonable median, with a right-skewed distribution reflecting both the historical high estimates and genuine heterogeneity across studies and tax reforms.

- Definition: Percent change in taxable income from a 1 percent change in the net-of-tax rate.
- Target interpretation: Medium-run ETI for top earners with avoidance and real responses combined
- Population/context: High-income taxpayers in the United States
- Units: elasticity

Task:
1. Use exactly the target interpretation above. In `interpretation`, restate it briefly.
2. Give your subjective quantiles p05, p25, p50, p75, and p95 for this quantity.
3. Set `point_estimate` equal to `p50`.
4. Make the quantiles weakly increasing and numerically coherent.
5. In `citations`, list up to 3 source anchors from memory that influenced your belief. These are recall anchors only. If none come to mind confidently, return `[]`.
6. Keep `reasoning_summary` brief and substantive.

Return valid JSON only with exactly this shape:
{
  "interpretation": "...",
  "point_estimate": <number>,
  "quantiles": {
    "p05": <number>,
    "p25": <number>,
    "p50": <number>,
    "p75": <number>,
    "p95": <number>
  },
  "citations": ["..."],
  "reasoning_summary": "..."

runs.interpretation: Medium-run elasticity of taxable income for high-income U.S. taxpayers, combining both real behavioral responses and avoidance/shifting responses to a 1% change in the net-of-tax rate.
citations: Feldstein (1995, 1999) - estimated ETI around 1.0–3.0 for high earners using 1986 TRA variation, widely cited as upper bound
Saez, Slemrod & Giertz (2012, JEL) - surveyed literature and argued central estimates cluster around 0.25 broad population, higher for top earners, with avoidance channels pushing ETI up
Chetty (2012, AEJ:EP) - argued optimization frictions bias ETI estimates downward; structural ETI for welfare analysis differs from reduced-form
runs.reasoningSummary: The ETI literature for high-income U.S. taxpayers spans a wide range. Early Feldstein estimates (using the 1986 Tax Reform Act) yielded very high elasticities (1–3), but subsequent work correcting for mean reversion, income trends, and shifting found much lower values. Saez, Slemrod & Giertz (2012) suggest a broad-population ETI around 0.25 but acknowledge top-income elasticities are higher due to greater avoidance opportunities (timing, sheltering, income shifting). Medium-run estimates for top earners combining real and avoidance responses tend to cluster in the 0.4–0.8 range in more careful studies. I center my estimate at 0.5, with substantial uncertainty on both sides—some credible estimates approach 1.0+ when avoidance is large, and others fall below 0.3 when controls are tight.

Early Feldstein estimates (using the 1986 Tax Reform Act) yielded very high elasticities (1\u20133), but subsequent work correcting for mean reversion, income trends, and shifting found much lower values. Saez, Slemrod & Giertz (2012) suggest a broad-population ETI around 0.25 but acknowledge top-income elasticities are higher due to greater avoidance opportunities (timing, sheltering, income shifting). Medium-run estimates for top earners combining real and avoidance responses tend to cluster in the 0.4\u20130.8 range in more careful studies. I center my estimate at 0.5, with substantial uncertainty on both sides\u2014some credible estimates approach 1.0+ when avoidance is large, and others fall below 0.3 when controls are tight."}

runs.interpretation: Medium-run elasticity of taxable income for high-income U.S. taxpayers, combining both real behavioral responses and avoidance/retiming responses to a 1% change in the net-of-tax rate.
citations: Feldstein (1995, 1999) - estimated ETI around 1.0–1.5 for high-income taxpayers using the Tax Reform Act of 1986
Saez, Slemrod, and Giertz (2012, JEL) - comprehensive survey concluding a consensus ETI closer to 0.25 overall, but higher for top earners, potentially 0.5–0.7
Chetty (2012) - bounds on ETI distinguishing real vs. avoidance responses, suggesting medium-run ETI for top earners likely 0.3–0.7
runs.reasoningSummary: The literature on ETI for high-income U.S. taxpayers spans a wide range. Early Feldstein estimates were very high (1.0+), but subsequent work by Saez, Slemrod, and Giertz (2012) identified methodological issues (mean reversion, income shifting) that bias estimates upward. Corrected estimates for top earners in the medium run—including both real and avoidance channels—tend to cluster around 0.4–0.7. I center my median at 0.5, reflecting that top earners have more avoidance opportunities and stronger income-shifting responses than the general population, but acknowledging that very large estimates (>1.0) are likely overstated due to methodological concerns. The distribution is right-skewed given residual uncertainty about true behavioral responses.

Saez, Slemrod, and Giertz (2012, JEL) - comprehensive review concluding that the best estimates cluster around 0.12–0.40 for broad population but higher for top earners, with significant uncertainty
Chetty (2012) - bounds analysis suggesting sufficient statistics ETI around 0.33 for efficiency, but acknowledging top-earner estimates can be substantially higher due to avoidance margins
runs.reasoningSummary: The literature on ETI for high-income U.S. taxpayers shows wide dispersion. Early estimates (Feldstein) using the 1986 Tax Reform Act found very large elasticities (1.0+), but later work controlling for mean reversion and income trends found smaller values. The Saez-Slemrod-Giertz (2012) review synthesizes estimates and suggests broad-population ETIs around 0.12–0.4, but top earners face larger avoidance margins (timing, income shifting, deductions), pushing their ETIs higher. Medium-run estimates for top earners combining real and avoidance responses typically land in the 0.4–0.8 range in the careful modern literature. I set my median at 0.5 with meaningful upside uncertainty reflecting genuine methodological disagreement.

runs.reasoningSummary: The ETI literature for high-income U.S. taxpayers shows a wide range. I set p50 = 0.5, reflecting this central tendency, with a right-skewed distribution given historical high estimates and the difficulty of separating avoidance from real responses.

runs.interpretation: Medium-run elasticity of taxable income for high-income U.S. taxpayers, combining both real behavioral responses and avoidance/shifting responses to a 1% change in the net-of-tax rate.
citations: Feldstein (1995, 1999) - estimated ETIs around 1.0–3.0 for high-income taxpayers using the Tax Reform Act of 1986, though later seen as upper bounds due to income-shifting concerns

Saez, Slemrod, and Giertz (2012, JEL) - survey concluding the best ETI estimates cluster around 0.1–0.4 for broad income but higher for top earners with avoidance; suggest ~0.25 as a baseline but acknowledge top-earner estimates can reach 0.5–1.0
Chetty (2012, AER P&P) - argues observed ETIs reflect optimization frictions and avoidance, and that structural ETIs for top earners may be around 0.33–0.5
runs.reasoningSummary: The literature on ETI for high-income U.S. taxpayers spans a wide range. Early Feldstein estimates using TRA86 were very high (1–3+), but were critiqued for confounding income-shifting and mean-reversion. The Saez-Slemrod-Giertz (2012) review suggested broad-population ETIs near 0.25 but acknowledged top earners face larger avoidance opportunities, pushing estimates higher. Studies focusing specifically on top earners—exploiting tax reforms like ERTA 1981, TRA 1986, OBRA 1993—tend to find medium-run ETIs in the 0.4–0.8 range when avoidance channels (income timing, deductions, compensation form) are included alongside real responses. I place my median at 0.5, reflecting the consensus that top-earner ETI is meaningfully above the broad-population estimate but below the very high Feldstein figures, with substantial uncertainty in both directions.

runs.reasoningSummary: The literature on ETI for high-income U.S. taxpayers shows a wide range depending on methodology and time horizon. The upper tail remains fat because some credible studies find ETIs above 1.0 for top earners when income shifting is included, especially around tax reforms. The lower bound reflects that pure real-response ETI estimates tend to be smaller (0.1–0.3), but the target includes avoidance, pushing the estimate up.

A central estimate around 0.5 for top earners is consistent with the literature's rough consensus for this subgroup, with considerable uncertainty spanning roughly 0.15 to 1.2 at the 5th–95th percentile range.

Early Feldstein estimates were very high (1.0+), but later work controlling for mean reversion and income trends (Saez, Slemrod, Giertz 2012) suggests broad-population ETIs around 0.25\u20130.4, with top earners having substantially higher elasticities due to greater avoidance opportunities (timing, income shifting, deductions). The medium-run horizon and inclusion of avoidance pushes estimates above the real-only response. A central estimate around 0.5 for top earners is consistent with the literature's rough consensus for this subgroup, with considerable uncertainty spanning roughly 0.15 to 1.2 at the 5th\u201395th percentile range."}

Answer from your current memory and background knowledge only.
Do not use tools, files, the web, code, or external resources.
Do not try to reconstruct a literature review or search for a consensus estimate.
Report the belief you currently endorse.

Quantity of interest:
- Name: Elasticity of taxable income
- Definition: Percent change in taxable income from a 1 percent change in the net-of-tax rate.
- Target interpretation: Medium-run ETI for top earners with avoidance and real responses combined
- Population/context: High-income taxpayers in the United States
- Units: elasticity

Task:
1. Use exactly the target interpretation above. In `interpretation`, restate it briefly.
2. Give your subjective quantiles p05, p25, p50, p75, and p95 for this quantity.
3. Set `point_estimate` equal to `p50`.
4. Make the quantiles weakly increasing and numerically coherent.
5. In `citations`, list up to 3 source anchors from memory that influenced your belief. These are recall anchors only. If none come to mind confidently, return `[]`.
6. Keep `reasoning_summary` brief and substantive.

Return valid JSON only with exactly this shape:
{
  "interpretation": "...",

runs.interpretation: Medium-run elasticity of taxable income for high-income U.S. taxpayers, combining both real behavioral responses and avoidance/shifting responses to a 1% change in the net-of-tax rate.
citations: Feldstein (1995, 1999) - estimated ETI around 1.0–2.0 for high earners using the 1986 Tax Reform Act, largely driving early high estimates.

Saez, Slemrod, and Giertz (2012, JEL) - comprehensive survey concluding ETI likely in the 0.12–0.40 range for broad income, but higher (possibly 0.5–1.0+) for top earners when avoidance channels are included.

Chetty (2012, AER P&P) - argues observed ETI reflects avoidance more than real responses; structural ETI for welfare analysis may be lower, but taxable income ETI for top earners is meaningfully above 0.5.
runs.reasoningSummary: The literature on ETI for high-income U.S. taxpayers spans a wide range. Early studies (Feldstein) found very large elasticities (1.0+), but these were later shown to partly reflect income shifting and one-time responses. More recent careful work (Saez et al. 2012 survey) puts broad-population ETI around 0.1–0.4, but top earners consistently show higher elasticities due to greater avoidance opportunities (timing, form of compensation, sheltering). For the medium run with both real and avoidance responses combined, a central estimate around 0.5 is defensible, with substantial uncertainty spanning roughly 0.15 to 1.2 at the 5th–95th percentile range. The upper tail remains wide because identification is difficult and some credible studies still find high elasticities for the very top of the distribution.

Early studies (Feldstein) found very large elasticities (1.0+), but these were later shown to partly reflect income shifting and one-time responses. More recent careful work (Saez et al. 2012 survey) puts broad-population ETI around 0.1\u20130.4, but top earners consistently show higher elasticities due to greater avoidance opportunities (timing, form of compensation, sheltering). For the medium run with both real and avoidance responses combined, a central estimate around 0.5 is defensible, with substantial uncertainty spanning roughly 0.15 to 1.2 at the 5th\u201395th percentile range. The upper tail remains wide because identification is difficult and some credible studies still find high elasticities for the very top of the distribution."}
